questions.question_text: Which one of the following is the most important reason that biomechanics is studied in the field of physical education and competitive sport today?

options.text: To improve athletic performance and reduce injury risk
To replace the role of the team coach permanently
To prepare the menu for athletes after every meal
To select the colour of the team uniforms only

questions.explanation: Biomechanics is studied to understand the mechanical principles behind human movement, allowing analysts to refine technique, improve athletic performance, design safer equipment and reduce injury risk through evidence-based intervention.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Biomechanics supports rather than replaces coaching.
Meal planning is sports nutrition, not biomechanics.
Uniform colour is unrelated to mechanical analysis.

questions.question_text: Rotation of a body segment around its own long axis, such as turning the forearm to face the palm down, is best described by which of the following terms?

options.text: Pronation of the forearm
Flexion of the elbow joint
Adduction of the shoulder
Plantarflexion of the foot

questions.explanation: Pronation is the rotation of the forearm around its long axis so that the palm faces downward; supination is the opposite movement bringing the palm to face upward, both essential to many sports skills like racquet handling.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Elbow flexion changes joint angle, not palm direction.
Shoulder adduction moves the arm toward the midline.
Plantarflexion describes ankle, not forearm, motion.

questions.question_text: The transverse (horizontal) plane of the body is best described as the plane that divides the body into which of the following parts during anatomical analysis?

options.text: Right and left halves of the body
Front and back portions of the body
Upper (superior) and lower (inferior) parts
Inner and outer surfaces of the limbs only

questions.explanation: The transverse or horizontal plane is parallel to the ground and divides the body into upper (superior) and lower (inferior) parts; rotational movements such as trunk twisting in batting and discus throw take place in this plane.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Right and left halves are produced by the sagittal plane.
Front and back portions arise from the frontal plane.
Inner-outer surfaces are not standard plane divisions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following body movements at the ankle joint is best described as plantarflexion of the foot during running and jumping activities in sport?

options.text: Bringing the toes upward toward the shin
Pointing the toes downward away from the shin
Turning the sole inward toward the midline
Rotating the leg around its long axis

questions.explanation: Plantarflexion is the movement of the foot at the ankle that points the toes downward away from the shin, like a sprinter pushing off at toe-off; the opposite movement, drawing toes toward the shin, is dorsiflexion.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Toes upward toward the shin is dorsiflexion.
Sole turning inward is inversion of the foot.
Rotation of the leg occurs at the hip, not ankle.

questions.question_text: Which of the following sport-specific scenarios best illustrates the principle that a wider base of support increases an athlete's stability against horizontal forces in play?

options.text: A goalkeeper standing with feet shoulder-width apart to face a free-kick
A gymnast balancing on a single tip-toe on the beam
A long-jumper sprinting on the runway just before take-off
A tennis player serving with one foot lifted off the ground

questions.explanation: A goalkeeper standing with feet shoulder-width apart enlarges the base of support; this places the line of gravity well within the base, increasing stability against the horizontal force of the ball impact and any opponent's challenge during a free-kick.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Single tip-toe markedly reduces the base of support.
Sprinting alternates feet on a very narrow base.
Lifting one foot during serving narrows the base.

questions.question_text: Which of the following changes will most likely raise the centre of gravity of a sprinter who is preparing to take off from a starting block in athletics?

options.text: Squatting low and tucking the head between the knees
Standing tall and raising both arms above the head
Lying flat on the ground with limbs spread out
Sitting cross-legged on the running track surface

questions.explanation: Raising the arms above the head shifts a significant portion of the body's mass upward, which lifts the centre of gravity; conversely, squatting, lying or sitting low all lower the centre of gravity below the standing position.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Squatting lowers the centre of gravity sharply.
Lying flat positions the body's mass closest to the ground.
Sitting cross-legged keeps the mass low and central.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why a high-jumper using the Fosbury flop technique can clear the bar even with the centre of gravity passing below the bar in a perfect attempt?

options.text: Because the centre of gravity always lies outside the human body
Because curving the body around the bar shifts mass parts to either side
Because gravity is suspended during a high-jump attempt
Because air resistance physically lifts the jumper upward

questions.explanation: In the Fosbury flop, the jumper arches the body around the bar so successive parts cross while others dip; this distributes mass on either side of the bar at any moment, allowing the centre of gravity to stay below the bar even as the body clears it.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The CG is usually inside, not outside, the body.
Gravity is never suspended during athletic events.
Air resistance lifts only highly streamlined projectiles, not jumpers.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes a key biomechanical advantage of using a third-class lever, such as the elbow biceps curl, in human limb movement?

options.text: Greater force output than the applied muscle effort
Greater speed and range of motion at the load end
Increased mechanical advantage greater than one
Complete elimination of any muscular work needed

questions.explanation: Third-class levers have effort applied between fulcrum and load and a mechanical advantage less than one; they trade off force for speed and range of motion at the load end, which is ideal for fast sporting actions like throwing and kicking.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Third-class levers produce less force than effort applied.
Their mechanical advantage is always less than one.
Muscular work cannot be eliminated by lever choice.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why a shot-putter benefits from a release angle of about 38–42° rather than the textbook 45° in the implement throwing event?

options.text: Because the textbook 45° angle does not exist in physics
Because the release height is well above the landing surface
Because shot-putters always have stronger left hands
Because release angles are randomly chosen by the thrower

questions.explanation: The classical 45° optimum assumes equal launch and landing heights; in shot-put the implement is released about two metres above the ground, so the optimal release angle drops to roughly 38–42° to maximise horizontal range under the height advantage.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The 45° optimum is correct under equal-height conditions.
Hand dominance does not change the projectile equation.
Release angles are based on biomechanics, not random choice.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most accurate biomechanical explanation for why a sprinter leans forward at the start of a 100-metre dash from blocks?

options.text: To deliberately reduce horizontal acceleration capacity
To shift centre of gravity ahead of feet, generating forward acceleration
To increase backward fall risk and slow the start
To prevent any leg muscle activation during the start phase

questions.explanation: Leaning forward shifts the centre of gravity ahead of the base of support, creating an unstable equilibrium that the sprinter resolves by accelerating the legs forward; this body geometry generates the desired horizontal acceleration phase off the blocks.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing acceleration capacity contradicts the sprint goal.
Backward fall risk would slow rather than speed the start.
Leg muscle activation is essential to drive off the blocks.

questions.question_text: Match each rotation-related body movement in Column A with its correct anatomical definition listed in Column B and select the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: Drawing toes upward toward the shin
Forearm rotated so palm faces downward
Forearm rotated so palm faces upward
Pointing toes downward away from shin

questions.explanation: Pronation rotates the forearm so the palm faces down; supination rotates so the palm faces up; dorsiflexion brings the toes up toward the shin; plantarflexion points the toes down away from the shin — four distinct body rotation movements.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Pronation is always palm-down rotation of the forearm.
Dorsiflexion specifically lifts the toes upward.
Plantarflexion always points the toes downward.

questions.question_text: Match each factor in Column A with its correct effect on the stability of a standing athlete listed in Column B and choose the option showing the right combination.

column_b.text: Causes the athlete to topple over

questions.explanation: A wider base of support increases stability of posture; a lower centre of gravity raises stability against pushes; the line of gravity falling outside the base causes toppling; greater body mass adds inertia, making the athlete harder to displace by external force.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Wider base genuinely improves postural stability.
Line of gravity outside the base always causes toppling.
Greater mass increases inertia and resistance to displacement.

questions.question_text: Match each sport-specific lever example in Column A with the correct lever class it represents in human biomechanics from Column B and choose the right combination.

column_a.text: Throwing a cricket ball with elbow extension by triceps
Calf raise standing on the balls of the feet
Biceps curl with a dumbbell in hand
Hamstring flexing the knee in football kick

column_b.text: Second-class lever (load between effort and fulcrum)
First-class lever (fulcrum between effort and load)

questions.explanation: Triceps elbow extension uses a first-class lever (fulcrum at elbow, effort posterior, load distal); calf raise is second-class; biceps curl is third-class at the elbow; hamstring knee flexion is third-class at the knee — distinct sporting lever examples.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Triceps elbow extension is a first-class lever.
Calf raise places body weight as load between fulcrum and effort.
Biceps curl and hamstring knee flexion are both third-class levers.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about widening the base of support and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in athletic stability.
questions.assertion: Spreading the feet wider apart increases the stability of an athlete in the frontal plane against side-on pushes.
questions.reason: A wider base of support allows the line of gravity to remain inside the base over a larger range of body sway angles.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: A wider base means the line of gravity must be displaced further before it crosses the edge of the base; this enlarges the safe sway range, directly explaining why side-on stability rises when the feet are placed further apart in the frontal plane.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason correctly explains the stability mechanism.
Both statements align with classical mechanics principles.
Wider feet directly raise sway tolerance in the frontal plane.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about lever systems in the human body and choose the option that best describes their logical connection in biomechanical analysis.
questions.assertion: Most of the lever systems in the human body during athletic movement are classified as third-class levers.
questions.reason: Third-class levers favour speed and range of motion at the load, which suit the demands of sporting actions like throwing and kicking.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Most muscle attachments place effort between fulcrum and load, creating third-class levers throughout the body; this arrangement sacrifices force for speed and range, perfectly suiting the explosive sporting demands of throwing, kicking and striking actions.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Third-class levers genuinely dominate body biomechanics.
The reason correctly explains the speed–range advantage.
Both statements are well-supported by anatomy.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about the line of gravity in standing posture and select the option showing their logical connection in postural stability.
questions.assertion: An athlete topples over the moment the line of gravity falls outside the base of support during balance challenges.
questions.reason: Once the line of gravity exits the base, the resulting torque about the edge of the base rotates the body about that edge.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: When the line of gravity exits the base of support, gravity creates a torque about the edge of the base that tips the body around that pivot; this torque mechanism directly explains the loss of equilibrium when the line of gravity moves outside the base.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason gives the correct torque mechanism.
Both statements are mechanically accurate.
Toppling is well-explained by edge-pivot rotation.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following events that occur during the throwing phase of a javelin attempt in the correct chronological order observed in athletic biomechanics analysis.

items.text: Acceleration of the javelin during the throwing arm action
Release of the javelin at optimal angle

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → R → S
S → R → P → Q
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: A javelin throw begins with an approach run building horizontal velocity, transfers momentum to the throwing arm during acceleration, releases the javelin at the optimal angle and finally completes the recovery follow-through to prevent foot fault and injury.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Approach run is the first event of any javelin throw.
Release follows acceleration of the throwing arm.
Follow-through is always the last phase of the throw.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages of a complete biomechanical analysis cycle of the high jump in the correct logical order followed by sports biomechanists in practice.

items.text: Identify performance goal and key variables
Capture multi-camera video of jump attempts
Analyse take-off angle, height, COG path

options.text: Q → R → S → P
R → Q → P → S
S → P → R → Q
P → S → Q → R

questions.explanation: A high-jump biomechanical analysis identifies the performance goal and key variables, captures multi-camera video of jump attempts, analyses take-off angle, height and centre-of-gravity path, and finally recommends technique modifications to the coach.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Goal identification logically precedes data capture.
Video must be captured before analysis can occur.
Coach feedback is always the closing recommendation step.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: The centre of gravity of an adult human in anatomical standing position can shift if the limbs move out of the neutral position.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: The centre of gravity is the average position of the body's mass, so any redistribution of mass through limb or trunk movement shifts its location; a high-jumper arching backwards even moves the CG outside the body for a brief moment.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Mass redistribution always shifts the centre of gravity.
Limb position changes are well-known to alter CG.
The Fosbury flop demonstrates large CG shifts in practice.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram of body movement directions and identify which movement is best described as bringing the toes upward toward the shin at the ankle joint during running.

options.text: Plantarflexion of the foot at the ankle
Dorsiflexion of the foot at the ankle
Inversion of the foot at the ankle
Eversion of the foot at the ankle

questions.explanation: Dorsiflexion is the movement that lifts the toes upward toward the shin at the ankle joint, important during the swing phase of running to clear the ground; the opposite movement, pointing the toes down, is plantarflexion.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Plantarflexion points toes downward, not upward.
Inversion turns the sole inward, not toes upward.
Eversion turns the sole outward, not toes upward.
